title: Learning to Think Like a Cartoon Captionist: Incongruity–Resolution Supervision for Multimodal Humor Understanding
research_questions: Can multimodal humor understanding be improved by supervising the intermediate incongruity-resolution reasoning process rather than treating caption selection as black-box prediction?

Do the three IRS components—incongruity modeling, resolution modeling, and preference alignment—improve New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest matching and ranking performance across 7B, 32B, and 72B backbones?
Do humor-specific reasoning traces and visual/style rewards transfer zero-shot to external visual humor benchmarks beyond NYCC?

datasets_used.name: Zhou et al. (2025) NYCC 10-vs-1000 and 30-vs-300 splits
Curated visual references for perception alignment
datasets_used.size: up to ten reference descriptions per cartoon

datasets_used.name: DeepEval
datasets_used.size: humorous subset (29 images, 2.9% of the benchmark)

methods_proposed.name: Incongruity-Resolution Supervision (IRS)
methods_proposed.summary: A three-stage framework for multimodal humor understanding that decomposes cartoon-caption reasoning into incongruity modeling, resolution modeling, and preference alignment. It is applied to Qwen2.5-VL backbones at 7B, 32B, and 72B scales.

methods_proposed.name: Captionist reasoning traces
methods_proposed.summary: Structured supervision traces for matching and ranking tasks that reconstruct the scene, identify salient visual incongruities, evaluate captions, and justify the final choice in a captionist-style format.

methods_proposed.name: Humor-aware preference alignment
methods_proposed.summary: A GRPO-based reinforcement learning stage combining accuracy and format rewards with visual perception and style rewards judged by Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, conditioned so perception and style rewards apply only when the answer is correct.

humor_theories.detail: IRS explicitly decomposes humor understanding into identifying mismatches in the visual scene and constructing coherent reinterpretations that resolve those mismatches.
Related work mentions script opposition as a perspective highlighting humor's structured cognitive nature.
Related work cites frame shifting as a perspective related to computational humor.
The paper discusses HOMER as a related caption generation framework grounded in the General Theory of Verbal Humor.

evaluation_methods.detail: Accuracy (%) is reported for NYCC caption matching, ranking, 10-vs-1000, and 30-vs-300 tasks, and for YesBut and DeepEval zero-shot evaluations.
The paper reports an expert captionist baseline and a non-expert user study with 21 participants on a subset of tasks.
Ranking labels use crowd-preferred captions and crowd-ranked caption ranges such as top-10 versus 1000–1009 and 30–39 versus 300–309.
Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct judges visual perception against curated visual references and style against five captionist criteria; these binary scores are used as reinforcement learning rewards.
Generated captionist reasoning traces are verified under human supervision for consistency with expert reasoning patterns.

headline_results.finding: IRS-72B achieved the highest ranking accuracy overall, surpassing all model baselines and approaching the expert captionist baseline on the Hessel et al. ranking task.
The strongest closed model, o3, achieved the best matching score overall and outperformed IRS-72B on matching.
IRS-7B improved over Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct on all four main NYCC-based settings.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker Caption Contest corpus / Zhou et al. (2025) NYCC splits

headline_results.finding: The full 7B IRS pipeline was the strongest 7B ablation across the reported tasks.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker Caption Contest corpus / Zhou et al. (2025) NYCC splits

headline_results.finding: Adding the visual perception reward to accuracy and format rewards improved the 7B IM+RM model most clearly on ranking-oriented settings.
headline_results.dataset: New Yorker Caption Contest corpus / Zhou et al. (2025) NYCC splits

headline_results.finding: IRS-7B transferred zero-shot to external visual humor benchmarks, substantially improving over Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct on both YesBut and DeepEval.

key_findings: Explicit incongruity-resolution supervision improves Qwen2.5-VL backbones at 7B, 32B, and 72B scales on NYCC caption matching and ranking tasks.
IRS-72B reaches 76.10% on the Hessel et al. ranking task, outperforming all model baselines in that column, including o3 at 62.85%.
Resolution Modeling is the main source of improvement in the 7B ablation, while Incongruity Modeling is helpful mainly when paired with structured reasoning supervision.
Preference Alignment adds complementary gains, especially for ranking tasks where visual grounding and stylistic quality matter.
Zero-shot results on YesBut and DeepEval suggest that IRS learns transferable visual humor reasoning patterns rather than only NYCC-specific heuristics.
The paper’s qualitative examples show that PA shifts outputs toward more grounded, concise, captionist-style explanations.
limitations: The model can still make visual perception errors on stylized cartoons, such as confusing a Viking warrior with the Grim Reaper, which then propagates into incorrect reasoning.
The model sometimes shows shallow cultural grounding, recognizing surface cues or over-attributing visual evidence without fully recovering the cultural script of a joke.
The authors note that reasoning supervision alone cannot compensate for systematic perception errors and that better vision encoders or domain-specific visual pretraining may be needed.
The data are centered on the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest and therefore reflect predominantly Anglophone and U.S.-centric humor conventions.
Humor evaluation remains subjective, and NYCC crowd judgments and editorial selections capture only a subset of possible human interpretations.
Parts of the IM corpus come from copyrighted sources such as books and podcast transcripts and cannot be directly released.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that the work is intended for research, creative assistance, education, and human–AI interaction rather than high-stakes decision-making or automated content moderation. It notes that humor is culturally dependent, may reflect biases in New Yorker-style data, and that humor-capable systems could be misused to generate offensive or inappropriate content.

artifacts.other: Project page: https://cyberiada.github.io/NYCC-Thinking/. The paper says evaluation train/test splits, preprocessing code, and prompt templates will be released, while copyrighted IM corpus materials cannot be directly released.
